data.epi_content:
I trudged weakly toward the front door.

I glanced at the mirror hanging beside it, straightened my clothes, and tidied my disheveled hair.

This is a kind of duty that any person should naturally fulfill.

No matter how haggard and sickly one becomes, a person should always maintain an air of dignity.

If someone comes to visit, one should show proper courtesy and at least maintain one's appearance so as not to make others uncomfortable.

Only fools who shouldn't even speak their family name would fail to do even that much.

What nonsense am I thinking? Ugh.

This is driving me crazy.

I should probably give up trying to distinguish between myself and this young lady inside me.

If I keep trying to draw a line between us in this strange situation where I've become her and she's become me, I feel like a fuse in my brain might just burn out.

Having your concepts and common sense turned upside down is quite an unpleasant feeling.

While I was lost in these thoughts, staring intently at myself in the mirror, I heard another knock at the door followed by a voice.

"Um, Erica! It's Vivian.

I came because I was worried. Could you please open the door?"

Right, the door. I was supposed to open the door.

I don't even have any drinks to offer my guest. Should I at least bring some water?

But then I worry that might lead to some new form of torment, that something terrible might happen to me again.

After taking a deep breath, I opened the door to find a pretty girl with blonde hair looking surprised.

As if she hadn't expected me to open it at all.

"Uh, um, uh, h-hello?"

After stammering in confusion for a moment, she suddenly greeted me.

I gave her a slight smile but didn't bother to respond.

My mind has been in complete disarray for a long time now, and all my feelings and thoughts about Evan, whom I once adored so much, have gone completely cold.

Perhaps that's why, even with this girl I used to despise standing right in front of me, I don't feel anything in particular.

It's as if I've become someone else... well, I have become someone else.

"Uh, um. Hmm..."

"Come in. I don't know why you're here, but come in."

Did she come to mock me?

To tell me that she's completely won Evan's heart and that I shouldn't even dream of getting close to him anymore?

Or maybe she came to return the slap I once gave her.

It's not like it would matter much if she hit me now.

I offered her a chair and searched the drawers for some cheap tea leaves, since I'd just finished the coffee.

Despite searching every corner, all I found was old dust.

After lightly dusting off my clothes, I looked at Vivian sitting there.

She seemed anxious, unable to keep still, fidgeting and scratching her cheeks here and there.

When I finally spoke, her shoulders, which had been relaxed until now, suddenly stiffened like a nervous new employee responding to a question.

"I'm sorry, but I don't have anything to offer you."

"N-no! It's, it's fine!"

"I see."

Then silence fell between us.

We weren't close, had nothing in common, and at least I wasn't in the mood to chat and laugh.
data.epi_content2:
Perhaps unable to bear the silence, the girl sitting across from me meekly handed me a small paper bag she was holding in her left hand.

I didn't take it.

She cleared her throat and placed it on the table.

"Well, I thought I shouldn't come empty-handed, so I baked some cookies..."

"Why did you come here?

If you're going to say strange things like you came because you were worried, please just leave."

Ah, this innocent girl.

Look at her adorable expression as she fumbles for words.

Even her troubled face is so beautiful—she was clearly blessed.

"I heard someone tried to harm you, and I overheard classmates gossiping. Since you haven't been coming to class, I was worried something might have happened..."

"Then..."

Before I could point to the door and tell her to leave, she gently placed her palm over my mouth.

For the first time, I understood what it feels like when your eyes widen in surprise.

She removed her hand and gave a slightly embarrassed smile as she stood up from the chair.

"It's true that I came because I was worried.

But I won't leave.

Because you look like you're about to cry."

Hearing that, I looked in the mirror.

My dull eyes were still empty, showing no emotion whatsoever, so I wondered what she was talking about.

Maybe she meant my tear ducts had dried up from keeping my eyes open for so long.

"Should I get down on my knees and apologize?

I'm sorry for slapping you, and I'm sorry for the harsh words.

But really, I just want to be alone."

Ignoring my words, the girl was picking up pieces of a broken glass from the floor and throwing them in the trash.

She must have cut her finger because she let out a small "ouch" and started sucking on her index finger.

"I'm not interested in Evan anymore.

Since we grew up together and were engaged or whatever, none of that matters anymore..."

My words might sound like nonsense to her.

Or perhaps they're just the mutterings of a now insignificant woman.

Having finished cleaning the floor, she went to the window and pulled aside the curtains that had been blocking all the sunlight.

Bright light began to fill the room.

And the girl smiling in that light looked so natural.

As if it were only natural for her to be bathed in such light, to be so beautiful, to be so charming—the way the light surrounded her and illuminated her face made it impossible to feel any confidence in one's own appearance.

"Um, should I call you 'my lady'?"

"There's no need to call me that anymore.

Haven't you heard? About what happened to my family."

"But you're still a lady.

I thought so from the first time we met.

Do you remember what I said when we first met?"

"Would you like to be friends with me?" Was that it?

This memory was clearer from when I read it as text rather than from the body's memories.

That was the first conversation with the character who would lose her fiancé to the protagonist.

Anyway, since I did remember, I nodded.

She smiled brightly.
data.epi_content3:
"When I first entered the Academy, you helped me with so many things.

When I didn't understand parts of the lessons, you and Evan would explain things or show me how to solve problems."

The young lady called Erica, destined to be a villain from birth, might have understood intellectually that marriage to a much prettier and more capable woman was more appealing than a marriage arranged by parents, but her heart simply couldn't accept it.

And no matter how she groomed herself, faced with this cute girl before her, she probably felt some negative emotion upon realizing she had nothing better to offer except her social status.

"Now we've kind of drifted apart..."

Even now, I could feel something rising within me—was it jealousy? Or hatred?

I'm not sure what to call it, but some dark emotion was slightly bubbling up.

Drifted apart? No.

From the beginning, Erica was just a medium to bring you and Evan together.

Seeming to realize she had misspoken, she stopped mid-sentence.

I didn't have the inclination or energy to say anything about it.

Well, it's not my concern anymore.

What matters is that if I don't start attending classes soon, I'll be expelled, and if I do go to class, I'll face all sorts of vile and malicious bullying.

Ah, should I ask this girl for help?

Just to have someone accompany me, to stop people from bothering me while I'm eating, to keep me away from those who try to boost their self-esteem by trampling on me.

If just these issues could be resolved, I think I could manage.

If I get kicked out of here right now, I'd have nowhere to go and no way to survive.

I'd like to just live off money without working, but the source of such funds has completely collapsed.

My lips moved slightly, then closed again.

Is it the emotional knot in my head, despite having no stake in this world?

Or is it useless pride already dissolved into this corpse?

For some reason, I just couldn't bring myself to ask this girl for help.

Feeling absurd, I let out a small laugh and sighed before speaking.

Why does she tense up so much at my slight laugh?

Ah, did I laugh like this before slapping her when she grabbed my sleeve?

It wasn't even that hard a slap... but there's no point in making excuses.

"Hey, Vivian."

"Yes."

"I'm really fine, so please leave.

We're not the kind of people who comfort each other, visit each other's rooms, or have conversations like this.

I'll be attending classes starting tomorrow anyway, so don't worry."

But she hesitated, repeating words like "but" and "well" and "if I do this."

When I didn't respond and just stared at her steadily, she got up and walked to the entrance.

And before opening the door to leave, she told me about the cookies.

"They're chocolate cookies made with milk.

Since you seem to like coffee... I brought something that would go well with it.

I'm sorry for coming by unannounced. I'll be going now."

With that, she left the room.

Why did she come?

We didn't even have any meaningful conversation.

I took out a cookie and put it in my mouth.

It was a sweet cookie with an exquisite combination of cocoa, but it tasted bland to me.
